claim: The high hapax ratio (70.1%) arises from a systematic abbreviation or suffix-stripping convention rather than from a large vocabulary, such that each Voynich 'word' represents a root plus a dropped inflectional ending â€” testable by checking whether hapax forms cluster around specific glyph-final patterns that would correspond to stripped suffixes.
evidence_for: 70.1% hapax ratio far exceeds natural languages (Latin ~40â€“50%, Italian ~45â€“55%)
Short average word length (5.03) is consistent with abbreviated forms
Word-final glyphs dominated by y/n/l/r/o could represent a small set of suffix surrogates
evidence_against: If suffixes were stripped uniformly, word entropy (10.45 bits) would likely be lower than observed
Currier A/B split implies different scribal conventions, complicating a single abbreviation rule
test: Cluster all 8,261 unique words by their final 1â€“2 glyphs; compute the type-token ratio within each final-glyph cluster and compare to the overall hapax rate. If abbreviation is the driver, hapax density should be uniform across final-glyph clusters rather than concentrated in rare final-glyph groups.
test_metric: Per-cluster hapax ratio vs. overall hapax ratio (70.1%); chi-square test of homogeneity across clusters
test_threshold: If no cluster deviates more than Â±10 percentage points from 70.1%, the abbreviation hypothesis gains support; deviation >20 pp in specific clusters would falsify it

tests_run.test: cluster all 8,261 unique words by their final 1â€“2 glyphs; compute the type-token ratio within each
tests_run.details: Observed hapax ratio: 0.701. Expected: ~0.50. Voynich has unusually high hapax ratio (70.1%), suggesting either large vocabulary, verbose encoding, or noise.

reasoning: Identifying whether hapax inflation is structural (suffix-stripping) or lexical (genuine large vocabulary) directly constrains whether the cipher encodes a morphologically rich language and whether root-extraction preprocessing is needed before decipherment
